Highlights
----------

*   •AM can overcome traditional limits in superconductor fabrication. 
*   •AM is transforming the production of superconducting materials. 
*   •Complex designs in superconductors are enabled from energy storage to electronics. 
*   •This integration can drive breakthroughs in various application fields. 

Abstract
--------

Additive manufacturing (AM) has revolutionised the production of materials and components. This review article introduces superconductivity and conventional fabrication methods, then overviews the progress and advancements in AM techniques applied explicitly to superconductors. It discusses the challenges and opportunities of AM methods like laser powder bed fusion (LPBF), electron beam melting (EBM), and extrusion-based approaches, examining their potential to overcome the limitations of conventional methods. The article highlights the applications reshaped by the AM and includes case studies showcasing how AM enables complex geometries for advanced superconducting components. Finally, the review explores the potential directions for further research and development in this field, emphasising the need for innovation and interdisciplinary collaboration. This review aims to serve as a valuable resource for researchers and engineers, offering insights into the significant impact of AM on advancing superconductivity technology.

1. Introduction
---------------

Superconductivity is a unique physical phenomenon where a material exhibits zero electrical resistance when cooled below a certain critical temperature, known as the superconducting transition temperature. This remarkable property enables the material to carry an electrical current with absolutely no energy loss, making superconductors indispensable in various advanced technological applications. Superconductors offer valuable and crucial possibilities for advanced technologies in various sectors, such as electric power, high-field magnets, energy, medicine, science, industry, and transportation [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0001)].

The conventional manufacturing of superconductors faces several hurdles, including the requirement for costly equipment, extended production timelines, limited design flexibility, constraints imposed by microstructure, and waste generation, which not only adds to the cost but also raises environmental concerns. Additionally, the rigid manufacturing processes limit design flexibility, which is crucial for optimising the performance and application of superconducting materials. The constraints imposed by the material's microstructure further complicate the production process, as the microstructural properties are closely tied to the superconducting performance. AM techniques offer several advantages over conventional methods, including the ability to precisely manipulate the microstructure of the material during fabrication, providing unprecedented control over the final properties of the superconductor. The inherent design flexibility of AM allows for the creation of complex geometries that are difficult or impossible to achieve with traditional manufacturing methods. This flexibility extends to the customisation of alloy compositions, enabling the integration of various elemental constituents in a single-step manufacturing process. However, the successful application of AM in the field of superconductivity requires more than just adopting existing AM techniques; it necessitates a comprehensive rethinking of the AM process itself. This includes the development of in-situ alloying techniques that can combine different elements during the AM process, ensuring that the resulting superconducting material meets or exceeds the performance standards of those produced by conventional means. Additionally, appropriate post-processing techniques must be implemented to further enhance the material properties, ensuring that the final product is suitable for high-performance applications. Beyond the direct fabrication of superconductors, AM technology can also play a supportive role in the production of applications associated with superconductivity [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0002),[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0003)]. AM can be used to create prototype models of superconducting components, allowing engineers and researchers to evaluate and refine their designs before committing to full-scale production. This capability not only reduces development time and costs but also opens up new possibilities for innovation, as designers can explore complex and unconventional geometries that were previously unattainable. For instance, fabricating prototype models of actual components to evaluate and refine their geometry (see [Fig. 8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#fig0008)(a)(b)) [[[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0004), [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0005), [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0006), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0007)].

To date, there have been limited efforts in leveraging AM to design and production of superconducting components. Despite these limited efforts, the potential benefits of AM, such as reduced time and cost in production, the ability to create intricate and innovative designs that were previously unimaginable, and the reduction of waste, should serve as strong incentives for advancing the field of superconductivity. Furthermore, the flexibility to modify designs on the go and the ongoing advancements in the industrialisation of AM and material characterisation will reveal even more noteworthy design advantages [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0008)]. Wragge-Morley et al. also tried to emphasise the potential benefits of using AM in the superconductivity field by giving examples such as novel designed topologies of the superconducting bus-bar joints, which demonstrate the versatility and potential of AM to innovate in this area [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0009)]. Currently, AM is mainly used for metal and polymer-based materials; however, the literature also expects an outlook of taking advantage of AM, specially fused deposition modelling, which is extrusion-based, to introduce superconducting materials in the field while improving the forming performance of multi-material ceramic components [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0010),[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0011)]. Even though the exploration of AM for superconductors is still in its early stages, the technology offers significant opportunities to overcome the limitations of conventional manufacturing methods. As AM continues to evolve and mature, it is poised to play a pivotal role in the advancement of superconductivity technology, driving innovation and enabling new applications that were previously beyond reach.

### 1.1. Theory of superconductivity

Most of the materials when they cool down to a specific temperature known as the "critical" temperature, undergo a transformation and enter a state called superconductivity (see [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#fig0001)(a)). In this state, two fundamental characteristics can be observed. (i); these materials exhibit zero resistance to the flow of electric current. This means that electrical current can circulate within the material without any energy loss. (ii); perfect diamagnetism (Meissner effect), if the external magnetic fields are not too strong, they are repelled by the surface of the superconductor [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0012)].

![Image 12: Fig. 1:](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0025540825001564-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (118KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0025540825001564-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (118KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0025540825001564-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. (a) Electrical resistivity vs temperature for normal conductors and superconductors, (b) Superconductivity region boundaries [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0013)].

The superconducting state is defined by certain limits represented by the critical temperature (T c), critical magnetic field (H c), and critical current density (J c) (see [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#fig0001)(b)) [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0013)]. These critical limits are dependent on each other related to external conditions. For instance, if the temperature rises, a decrease in H c and J c will occur. In other words, when a superconductor is cooled further below the T c, it will provide higher H c and J c values. In case of an increase in the applied magnetic field, the J c decreases. [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#fig0001)(b) represents the superconductivity region, showing the borders of these critical parameters that the materials scientists are trying to expand [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0014)]. When the current density is below J c, the flow of current occurs without any resistance or power loss. However, if the current density surpasses J c, a voltage arises across the superconductor, breaking the zero resistance condition [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0015)].

T c and H c of a superconducting system are intrinsic properties and primarily determined by its chemistry and physics, and they remain relatively unaffected by the processing techniques applied to the superconductor. However, this is not the case for the J c, which can undergo significant changes by altering the fabrication process and, consequently, the microstructure of the material. For instance, in the Nb-Ti alloy system, the values of T c and H c are essentially fixed once the alloy composition is determined. However, through various metallurgical treatments during the processing of the alloy, the J c can be modified by factors of 1k or more [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0016)].

Superconductors that completely prevent the penetration of an applied magnetic flux density are referred to type-I superconductors [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0017)]. For type-I, the superconductivity behaviour is destroyed when the magnetic flux density exceeds the threshold level. Type-II superconductors, like type-I superconductors, exhibit excellent electrical conductivity without any resistance. However, their magnetic behaviour is more intricate. When the applied magnetic field is low, type-II superconductors completely expel magnetic flux. However, as the applied field increases, they only partially exclude the flux. In the presence of higher magnetic fields, their diamagnetic properties are not perfect but rather demonstrate a mixed type of response. The value of J c is influenced by the movement of flux lines that penetrate the material. This motion of flux lines generates heat, causing the material to lose its superconductivity by surpassing T c. However, these flux lines can be pinned down by intentionally introducing defects such as grain boundaries, dislocations, impurities, or an additional phase with normal conducting properties. This pinning mechanism maintains the material's superconductivity and prevents the heat generation that would otherwise occur [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0018)]. In an ideal scenario, the optimal microstructure would consist of a homogeneous matrix along with carefully aligned pinning centres.

The superconducting critical current density plays a vital role in developing and constructing practical devices utilising superconductors. It is the primary parameter of utmost significance in various applications. This is mainly because superconducting wires are commonly used to build electromagnets, which generate their magnetic field based on the ampere-turns within the magnet winding. Numerous electromagnets available today rely on this principle, such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) magnets, research magnets operating at high magnetic fields, magnets used for beam-bending, focusing, and detection in high-energy physics research, superconducting energy storage systems, as well as superconducting motors and generators [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0019)]. When the critical current density of a material rises, it leads to an increase in the magnetic field that can be achieved while simultaneously reducing the amount of superconductor required to build the magnet. As a result, there is a clear motivation to enhance the critical current density of the superconductors [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0015)].

### 1.2. Additive manufacturing and techniques

There is currently a wide range of AM methods accessible, which vary in how they deposit layers to fabricate parts, their underlying principles of operation, and the materials they can utilise. These methods play a crucial role in the fabrication of complex geometries that are often unachievable with conventional manufacturing techniques. AM techniques can be broadly classified into three primary categories: powder-based, extrusion-based, and adhesive-based methods [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0020)]. So far, mostly LPBF and EBM techniques under powder-based, along with extrusion-based methods, have been predominantly utilised in superconductor-related research and development.

In AM, the process involves the sequential addition of material, layer by layer, until the entire geometry of the designed part is constructed. This layer-wise approach is not only fundamental to the definition of AM but also allows for the creation of intricate internal structures and overhangs that would be impossible or highly impractical to achieve with traditional manufacturing. The dimensions of the part to be printed, including its length, width, and height, must be carefully designed to fit within the build envelope of the selected AM machine. This necessitates a thoughtful consideration of the AM system's capabilities and limitations during the design phase.

The design process itself is typically carried out using Computer-Aided Design (CAD) software, which allows for precise control over the geometry of the part. Once the design is finalised, it is essential to convert the CAD model into a format that can be interpreted by the AM machine. This is done using slicing software, which translates the 3D model into a series of 2D cross-sectional layers, or slices, that the machine will sequentially build. The choice of slicing software is dependent on the specific AM technology being used, as different machines may require different file formats and settings.

Designing for AM involves unique challenges that are not encountered in traditional manufacturing processes. One of the key considerations is the need to manage overhang portions of the structure that extend outwards without direct support beneath them. These overhangs require the strategic placement of supports during the printing process to prevent deformation or collapse. The supports, which are typically made of the same material as the part or a secondary material that can be easily removed, must be carefully designed and positioned to ensure the structural integrity of the part during fabrication. After the part is printed, these supports must be removed, a process that can vary in complexity depending on the material and AM method used.

The choice of building substrate is another critical factor in the AM process. The substrate not only serves as the initial layer upon which the part is built but also affects the thermal and mechanical properties of the final part. For instance, in methods like LPBF and EBM, the substrate must be compatible with the metal powders used and capable of withstanding the high temperatures generated during the melting process. In some cases, it may be necessary to adjust the substrate's temperature to improve adhesion between the first layer and the substrate. Increasing the temperature of the substrate can also be used to manipulate the resulting microstructure in terms of grain size, phases, etc., as it affects the heat distribution and hence decreases the solidification speed [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0021)].

In powder-based AM methods, such as LPBF and EBM, the process begins with the distribution of a fine layer of metal powder over the substrate. This is typically done using a coater system, which ensures an even distribution of the powder across the build area. Following the powder distribution, a laser or electron beam selectively melts the powder according to the design specified by the slicing software. The precision and control of the beam are paramount, as they determine the resolution and quality of the final part. LPBF and EBM processes are conducted in an inert gas atmosphere, usually argon or nitrogen, within a sealed chamber. This controlled environment is crucial for preventing oxidation or contamination of the metallic powders, which could compromise the mechanical properties of the final part.

Extrusion-based AM technologies, on the other hand, operate by forcing material through a nozzle to build the part layer by layer. This method offers several advantages, including greater flexibility in design, the ability to construct larger parts, and cost-effectiveness compared to powder-based processes. Extrusion-based techniques are particularly advantageous for materials that may not be well-suited for powder-based methods, such as those with high melting points or inherent brittleness, like ceramics. To make these materials more suitable for extrusion, they are often blended with polymers or other binders that improve their flow properties during the extrusion process [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0022)]. The ability to tailor the material properties through such blending opens up a wide range of possibilities for producing parts with customised properties that meet specific application requirements.

Each AM method has its own set of advantages, limitations, and specific applications, making the selection of the appropriate technique a critical decision in the manufacturing process. As the field of AM continues to evolve, ongoing research and development are expected to further expand the capabilities of these technologies, particularly in the realm of superconductors, where precision, material properties, and intricate geometries are of significant importance.

2. Manufacturing methods of superconductors
-------------------------------------------

### 2.1. Conventional manufacturing of superconductors

Various materials, such as pure metals, alloys, organic substances, polymers, and even substances that typically do not conduct electricity, have demonstrated the phenomenon of superconductivity. At present, at least 27 naturally existing elements exhibit superconductivity when cooled to extremely low temperatures under normal atmospheric pressure. Furthermore, the number of alloys and intermetallic compounds that possess this property exceeds 1000 [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0015),[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0023)]. The practical superconductors can be classified into two main categories depending on the cooling media used to reach below their T c; liquified Nitrogen (77 K) for high-temperature superconductors (HTS) and liquified Helium (4.2 K) for low-temperature superconductors (LTS). LTS group can also be split into solid-solution superconductors and A15 superconductors. NbTi and MgB 2 are some examples of a solid-solution superconductor that can go up to around a magnetic field of 9T and 13T [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0024)], respectively, whereas the A15 superconductors can reach values of 20T To benefit from these high magnetic fields, A15 superconductors are made into wires and tapes using various methods. However, the main drawback of the A15 superconductors is that they are very brittle and need to be combined with other materials to give the final shape, and the applied heat treatments during the manufacturing steps generate the superconducting properties. These alloys are mainly formed by niobium and vanadium combined with tin, aluminium, germanium or gallium individually, like Nb 3 Sn, Nb 3 Al, Nb 3 Ga, Nb 3 Ge, V 3 Al, V 3 Ga, etc. There are many different methods to produce these superconductors, such as diffusion process, powder metallurgy route, bronze process, internal Sn process, jelly roll process, tube process, which are designed specifically to manufacture wire/tape forms due to the brittle nature of these alloys [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0025540825001564#bib0015)]. In this section, we will briefly explain the conventional manufacturing routes of the selected superconductors.

Conventionally the superconductors can be manufactured in the forms of multi-filamentary round wire, multi-filamentary tape, multi-layered tape (coated conductor), bulk pellet and thin film. Widely used applications require wire-type superconductors and are commonly made around 1 mm as the outer diameter contains a high number of superconducting filaments in micron sizes distributed systematically along the matrix. ## Abstract

Single-crystal microstructures enable high-performance YBa2Cu3O7-x superconductors which are however limited to simple shapes due to their brittleness. Additive manufacturing can fabricate YBa2Cu3O7-x superconductor with complex shapes, albeit with a polycrystalline microstructure. Here, we demonstrate a route to grow single-crystals from 3D-ink-printed, polycrystalline, sintered superconducting YBa2Cu3O7-x (YBCO or Y123) + Y2BaCuO5 (Y211), manufacturing objects with complex architectures displaying both high critical current density (Jc=2.1 × 104 A.cm–2, 77 K) and high critical temperature (Tc= 88-89.5 K). An ink containing precursor powders (Y2O3, BaCO3, and CuO) is 3D-extruded into complex geometries and then reaction-sintered to obtain polycrystalline Y123 + Y211. A seed is then utilized to transform these 3D-printed parts from polycrystal to monocrystal via the melt growth method. The geometric details of 3D-printed parts survive the process without slumping, sagging or collapse, despite the long-term presence of liquid above the peritectic temperature. Origami structures can be created by sheet folding after 3D-printing. This additive approach enables the facile fabrication of superconducting devices with complex shapes and architectures, such as advanced undulator magnets to generate synchrotron radiation and microwave cavities for dark-matter axion search. This work highlights the potential of additive manufacturing for producing monocrystalline cuprate superconductors and opens the door to additive manufacturing of other monocrystalline functional ceramic or semiconductor materials.

## Introduction

YBa2Cu3O7-x (YBCO or Y123) is the original high-temperature cuprate oxide with a superconducting critical temperature (Tc~93 K) well above the boiling point of liquid nitrogen (77 K)[1](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR1 "Wu, M. K. et al. Superconductivity at 93 K in a new mixed-phase Y-Ba-Cu-O compound system at ambient pressure. Phys. Rev. Lett. 58, 908–910 (1987)."), enabling various transformational applications, both for existing (e.g., magnetic resonance imaging, nuclear magnetic resonance) and future (e.g., energy storage, fusion power plants[2](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR2 "Molodyk, A. et al. Development and large volume production of extremely high current density YBa2Cu3O7 superconducting wires for fusion. Sci. Rep. 11, 2084 (2021)."), maglev trains)[3](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR3 "Namburi, D. K., Shi, Y. & Cardwell, D. A. The processing and properties of bulk (RE)BCO high temperature superconductors: current status and future perspectives. Supercond. Sci. Technol. 34, 053002 (2021)."). Additive manufacturing of monocrystalline YBCO superconductors not only offers greater design flexibility for existing applications but also paves the way for new innovations. The architectural freedom of additive manufacturing enables optimized magnetic field distribution, efficient cooling to sustain the superconducting state, and a lightweight structure for levitation applications. Additive manufacturing of ceramics, like YBCO, currently produces only a polycrystalline microstructure when followed by conventional sintering. However, grain boundaries are weak links for current transport in YBCO, principally because the coherence length (related to the characteristic size of a Cooper pair) of YBCO is too short to allow supercurrent to span over the structural disorder caused by a grain boundary[4](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR4 "Durrell, J. et al. The behavior of grain boundaries in the Fe-based superconductors. Rep. Prog. Phys. 74, 124511 (2011)."). Therefore, YBCO with a polycrystalline microstructure typically has a low critical current density (~5 × 101A.cm–2, 77 K, zero field)[5](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR5 "Zhang, B. et al. Efficient fabrication of ultralight YBa2Cu3O7−x superconductors with programmable shape and structure. Adv. Funct. Mater. 31, 2100680 (2021).") due to the presence of numerous grain boundaries. In contrast, forming a single-crystal microstructure effectively eliminates these grain boundaries, leading to a significant increase in critical current density ( ~ 4.5 × 104A.cm-2, 77 K, H | |c, zero field)[6](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR6 "Namburi, D. K. et al. A novel, two-step top seeded infiltration and growth process for the fabrication of single grain, bulk (RE)BCO superconductors. Supercond. Sci. Technol. 29, 095010 (2016)."). Therefore, YBCO superconductors are mainly used as coated tapes/wires where the thin YBCO film is epitaxially grown on a biaxially textured conductor substrate[7](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR7 "Dam, B. et al. Origin of high critical currents in YBa2Cu3O7−δ superconducting thin films. Nature 399, 439–442 (1999).") which can achieve a high critical current density (more than 3 × 106A/cm2 at 77 K 3). However, these ceramic-like tapes cannot be bent over a radius below 2–10 mm without degrading their critical current[8](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR8 "Richter, S. C. et al. JACoW: Bending radius limits of different coated REBCO conductor tapes-an experimental investigation with regard to HTS undulators. JACoW IPAC 21, 3837–3840 (2021)."), nor can they be shaped in complex 3D-objects. Furthermore, even for superconducting tapes, joints between strands to achieve longer length or larger width induce residual resistance[9](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR9 "Shin, H. S., Nisay, A., MBd, Leon & Dedicatoria, M. J. Joining of REBCO coated conductor tapes using ultrasonic welding method. IEEE Trans. Appl. Superconductivity 25, 1–5 (2015)."). This severely limits applications where complex 3D architectures are needed, such as microwave cavities with minimal gaps between tapes[10](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR10 "Ahn, D. et al. Biaxially textured YBa2Cu3O7-x microwave cavity in a high magnetic field for a dark-matter axion search. Phys. Rev. Appl. 17, L061005 (2022).") and undulator with short periods[8](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR8 "Richter, S. C. et al. JACoW: Bending radius limits of different coated REBCO conductor tapes-an experimental investigation with regard to HTS undulators. JACoW IPAC 21, 3837–3840 (2021)."). In bulk form, YBCO (specifically: Y123 + Y2BaCuO5 (Y211)) single crystals (single grain or single domain) can be grown by the top-seeded melt growth or top-seeded infiltration growth method[3](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR3 "Namburi, D. K., Shi, Y. & Cardwell, D. A. The processing and properties of bulk (RE)BCO high temperature superconductors: current status and future perspectives. Supercond. Sci. Technol. 34, 053002 (2021)."). These bulk YBCO single crystals can trap large magnetic fields[11](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR11 "Namburi, D. K., Shi, Y., Dennis, A. R., Durrell, J. H. & Cardwell, D. A. A robust seeding technique for the growth of single grain (RE)BCO and (RE)BCO–Ag bulk superconductors. Supercond. Sci. Technol. 31, 044003 (2018).") and can potentially substitute iron-based permanent magnets, e.g., for superconducting motors[12](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR12 "Grilli, F. et al. Superconducting motors for aircraft propulsion: The advanced superconducting motor experimental demonstrator project. J. Phys.: Conf. Ser. 1590, 012051 (2020).") or generators[13](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR13 "Jensen, B. B., Mijatovic, N. & Abrahamsen A. B. Development of superconducting wind turbine generators. J. Renew. Sustain. Energy 5, 023137 (2013)."). However, they are currently limited to simple cylinder or cuboid shapes.

3D-ink-printing, an additive manufacturing (AM) method, extrudes a powder-loaded ink, layer by layer, and the resulting green part is then sintered to achieve densification[14](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR14 "Jakus, A. E., Taylor, S. L., Geisendorfer, N. R., Dunand, D. C. & Shah, R. N. Metallic architectures from 3D-printed powder-based liquid inks. Adv. Funct. Mater. 25, 6985–6995 (2015)."),[15](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR15 "Lewis, J. A., Smay, J. E., Stuecker, J. & Cesarano, J. Direct ink writing of three-dimensional ceramic structures. J. Am. Ceram. Soc. 89, 3599–3609 (2006)."). The isothermal sintering and subsequent slow cooling prevent the formation of thermal-shock induced cracks in ceramics, which are frequently observed in laser-beam-based AM methods[16](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR16 "Chen, Z. et al. 3D printing of ceramics: A review. J. Eur. Ceram. Soc. 39, 661–687 (2019)."). Also, ink can utilize submicron and nano-particles of various shapes and size distribution, unlike powder-bed-based AM methods (binder/ink jet printing[17](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR17 "Lv, X., Ye, F., Cheng, L., Fan, S. & Liu, Y. Binder jetting of ceramics: Powders, binders, printing parameters, equipment, and post-treatment. Ceram. Int 45, 12609–12624 (2019).")) for which powder flowability is limiting[18](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR18 "Meier, C., Weissbach, R., Weinberg, J., Wall, W. A. & Hart, A. J. Critical influences of particle size and adhesion on the powder layer uniformity in metal additive manufacturing. J. Mater. Process Technol. 266, 484–501 (2019)."). Previously, only polycrystalline Y123 superconductors have been fabricated by 3D ink-printing[5](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR5 "Zhang, B. et al. Efficient fabrication of ultralight YBa2Cu3O7−x superconductors with programmable shape and structure. Adv. Funct. Mater. 31, 2100680 (2021)."),[19](#ref-CR19 "Wei, X. et al. Extrusion printing of a designed three-dimensional YBa2Cu3 O7− x superconductor with milled precursor powder. J. Mater. Chem. C. 5, 3382–3389 (2017)."),[20](#ref-CR20 "Wei, X. et al. Fabrication of YBa2Cu3O7−x (YBCO) superconductor bulk structures by extrusion freeforming. Ceram. Int 42, 15836–15842 (2016)."),[21](#ref-CR21 "Mendes, D. et al. Low-cost and high-performance 3D printed YBCO superconductors. Ceram. Int 47, 381–387 (2021)."),[22](#ref-CR22 "Zhang, B., Zhang, X. & Zhou, Y.-H. Lightweight, highly tough and durable YBa2Cu3O7–x superconductor. Natl. Sci. Rev. 10, nwad030 (2023)."),[23](#ref-CR23 "Ma, Y., Zhang, B., Zhang, X. & Zhou, Y.-H. Controllable rectification on the thermal conductivity of porous YBa2Cu3O7−x superconductors from 3D-printing. Int J. Extrem. Manuf. 5, 015001 (2023)."),[24](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR24 "Coveney A., Morrison K., Engstrøm D. DC Resistance measurements in multi-layer additively manufactured yttrium barium copper oxide components. Adv Eng Mater 26, 2300773 (2023)."). These polycrystalline microstructures are unable to create/trap a strong magnetic field or achieve heavy-load levitation.

Here, we develop and demonstrate a approach, where a single crystal is grown from a 3D-ink-printed Y123 + Y211 architectures with an excellent shape fidelity, achieving a nearly hundred-fold improvement in critical current density as compared to prior polycrystalline Y123 objects created by AM.

## Results and discussion

### Sintering

A powder-loaded ink - containing a blend of submicron Y2O3 + BaCO3 + CuO precursor powders (Fig. [S1](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#MOESM1)) with a binder (PLGA), solvent (DCM), plasticizer (DBP), and surfactant (EGBE) - is extruded layer by layer from a 250 μm diameter nozzle to fabricate complex parts such as a toroidal coil, as shown in Fig. [1a](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#Fig1). The rapid solvent evaporation after ink extrusion causes the binder to precipitate immediately after extrusion thereby increasing the strength of the deposited material, unlike non-evaporating ink systems. As a result, this ink system does not show slumping and sagging of the printed filaments[25](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR25 "Geisendorfer, N. R. & Shah, R. N. Effect of polymer binder on the synthesis and properties of 3D-printable particle-based liquid materials and resulting structures. ACS Omega 4, 12088–12097 (2019).") and thus enables them to partially overhang and form the arcs in the toroidal coil without auxiliary supports. After printing, a relatively dense microstructure is needed to keep the integrity of the printed structure during the subsequent single-crystal growth step. Also, high densities minimize porosity near the growth front of the single crystal, enabling fast single-crystal growth. However, the powder-loaded ink consists of loosely packed powders, making densification challenging. The composition targeted - pure YBa2Cu3O7-x (Y123) - is first used to study the synthesis and sintering behavior. As shown in Fig. [1b](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#Fig1), the relative mass of the extruded ink decreases between 50 and 350 °C, which corresponds to the decomposition of the PLGA binder. The atmosphere for debinding is selected as Ar-1 mol.% O2 to prevent cracking caused by binder combustion (Fig. [S2](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#MOESM1)). After debinding, another mass loss occurs between 750 and 900 °C, corresponding to the decomposition of BaCO3 releasing CO2. From the in-situ X-ray diffraction data (Fig. [1c](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#Fig1)), the BaCO3 transforms from the orthorhombic to the trigonal phases at ~700 °C. Thereafter, the tetragonal Y123 phase starts to form, while the decomposition of BaCO3 continues. The diffraction peaks for BaCO3 disappear at ~780 °C, and the peaks of Ba1-yCuO2+δ ∙ (CO2)x[26](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR26 "Peitola, K. et al. Structural changes related to changes in oxygen stoichiometry in the Ba1–yCuO2+ δ·(CO2)x system as determined by powder X-ray and neutron diffraction. J. Mater. Chem. 9, 2599–2603 (1999).") (Ba-Cu-O-CO2) appear subsequently, consistent with the TGA results showing that CO2 release is not complete until a temperature of 900 °C is reached. After full CO2 release, the barium copper oxycarbonate is expected to transform to BaCuO2, which forms a transient liquid phase above ~970 °C[27](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR27 "Krabbes G., Fuchs G., Canders W.-R., May H. & Palka R. High temperature superconductor bulk materials. NJ, Hoboken: Wiley-VCh Darmstadt, (2006)."), thus facilitating the densification process. Therefore, a sintering temperature of 1000 °C is selected after comparing with an experiment carried out at 985 °C (Fig. [S3](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#MOESM1)). After sintering at 1000 °C for 20 h, the micro-lattice sample exhibits an orthorhombic Y123 phase (Fig. [1d](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#Fig1)). The micro-lattices, before and after sintering (Fig. [1e](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#Fig1)), show a uniform linear shrinkage of 34.7 ± 0.3 % without cracks or warpage. The densified polycrystal microstructures - with a relative density of 89 ± 5 % and a grain size of 1.4 ± 0.6 μm - are shown in Fig. [1f](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#Fig1).

### Single-crystal growth

For single-crystal growth, the ink composition is altered to achieve a Y-rich, two-phase composition (69 wt.% YBa2Cu3O7-x [superconducting Y123] + 30 wt.% Y2BaCuO5 [non-superconducting Y211] + 1 wt.% CeO2, labeled in the following Y123 + Y211) where (i) the Y211 phase provides additional Y to the Y-depleted melt near the growth front[28](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR28 "Zhai, W., Shi, Y., Durrell, J. H., Dennis, A. R. & Cardwell, D. A. The influence of Y−211 content on the growth rate and Y-211 distribution in Y–Ba–Cu–O single grains fabricated by top seeded melt growth. Cryst. Growth Des. 14, 6367–6375 (2014).") and (ii) CeO2 helps refine the Y211 particles by reacting with Y211 to form Y₂O₃, BaCeO₃, and CuO[29](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR29 "Piñol, S. et al. Enhanced critical currents by CeO2 additions in directionally solidified YBa2Cu3O7. Appl Phys. Lett. 65, 1448–1450 (1994)."). The newly formed Y₂O₃ particles can act as nucleation sites for Y211, ultimately leading to the formation of smaller, finely-sized Y211 particles[29](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR29 "Piñol, S. et al. Enhanced critical currents by CeO2 additions in directionally solidified YBa2Cu3O7. Appl Phys. Lett. 65, 1448–1450 (1994)."), providing more pinning centers for a higher critical current density Jc[29](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR29 "Piñol, S. et al. Enhanced critical currents by CeO2 additions in directionally solidified YBa2Cu3O7. Appl Phys. Lett. 65, 1448–1450 (1994)."),[30](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR30 "Kim, C.-J. et al. Formation of BaCeO3 and its influence on microstructure of sintered/melt-textured Y-Ba-Cu-O oxides with CeO2 addition. J. Mater. Res 9, 1952–1960 (1994)."). Kim et al. demonstrated that adding 1 wt.% CeO₂ to Y123-Y211 can double the critical current density from 1.1 to 2.0 × 10⁴ A/cm² at 77 K under a magnetic field of 1 T[30](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR30 "Kim, C.-J. et al. Formation of BaCeO3 and its influence on microstructure of sintered/melt-textured Y-Ba-Cu-O oxides with CeO2 addition. J. Mater. Res 9, 1952–1960 (1994)."). After sintering at 1000 °C/ 20 h, the sample shows a densified microstructure with a relative density of 78 ± 1 % and a linear shrinkage of 28.3 ± 0.4 % (Fig. [2a, b](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#Fig2)). The sintered micro-lattice is then heated above its peritectic temperature to decompose the Y123 phase to the Y211 + (Ba,Cu-rich) liquid phase[27](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR27 "Krabbes G., Fuchs G., Canders W.-R., May H. & Palka R. High temperature superconductor bulk materials. NJ, Hoboken: Wiley-VCh Darmstadt, (2006)."). Cooling is performed at a very slow rate (0.5 K/ h) which, upon crossing the peritectic temperature, triggers single-crystal growth initiated from a NdBa2Cu3O7-x (NdBCO) single-crystal thin film seed. The formation of a liquid phase during the singe-crystal growth is expected to remove the residual pores by filling the pores and rearranging Y211 particles. A nearly fully densified microstructure is indeed observed (Fig. [2b](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#Fig2)), consisting of a Y123 matrix in which Y211 and BaCeO3 particles are embedded. Despite liquid formation during the singe-crystal growth, the two printed micro-lattices kept their overall shape and printed details with high fidelity. A small extent of warpage is observed, but it is limited to the 2-3 printed layers (~400 μm) that are close to the seed (Fig. [2e](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#Fig2)).

Insert shows Y211 and BaCeO3 particles in the Y123 matrix. **c** XRD spectra for the top and bottom faces of the 3D-printed lattice after single-crystal growth, showing single c-axis orientation on both faces. **d** Higher magnification IPF (inverse pole figure) map (left) and phase map (right) showing distribution of the Y211 phase (green) in the Y123 phase (gray). **e** Stitched IPF maps of full vertical cross-section on the side face of the 3D-printed lattice, showing \(\{100\}/\{010\}\) orientation (single crystal), using a cubic version of Y123 for indexing. **f** Enlarged SEM-BSE micrographs showing Y211 concentration at convergence planes (circled), following the growth of the single crystal (marked with dotted lines).

Remarkably, the 3D-printed micro-lattice maintains its original shape without slumping or collapsing, despite the large amount of liquid phase needed for single-crystal growth. According to percolation theory, an interconnected 3D solid skeleton starts to form when the volume fraction of jammed hard spheres is above 18.3%[31](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR31 "Powell, M. J. Site percolation in randomly packed spheres. Phys. Rev. B 20, 4194–4198 (1979)."). This percolation threshold decreases when the solid particles are sphero-cylinders[32](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR32 "White, S. I. et al. Electrical percolation behavior in silver nanowire–polystyrene composites: Simulation and experiment. Adv. Funct. Mater. 20, 2709–2716 (2010)."): White et al. found that silver nanowires with an aspect ratio of 8 in polystyrene composite have a percolation threshold of 8.3%[32](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR32 "White, S. I. et al. Electrical percolation behavior in silver nanowire–polystyrene composites: Simulation and experiment. Adv. Funct. Mater. 20, 2709–2716 (2010)."). The solid Y211 particles exhibit both sphere and cylinder-like shapes on the surface of the lattice (Fig. [S4](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#MOESM1)), which is similar to the observation from quenching experiments above the peritectic temperature[30](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR30 "Kim, C.-J. et al. Formation of BaCeO3 and its influence on microstructure of sintered/melt-textured Y-Ba-Cu-O oxides with CeO2 addition. J. Mater. Res 9, 1952–1960 (1994)."). If solid Y211 particles are inter-penetrable during sintering, a percolation threshold of 15.6% is obtained for an aspect ratio of 3 for the Y211 particles (Supplementary Information SI[1](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#MOESM1)). From the vertical section of the Y2O3–BaO–CuO phase diagram (Fig. [S4](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#MOESM1)), the volume fraction of the solid Y211 phase is ~36% at the temperature of 1090 °C. Therefore, a continuous Y211 skeleton can be expected to form above the peritectic temperature, thus preventing the slumping of the microlattice struts. In addition, various studies of top-seeded infiltration growth[3](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR3 "Namburi, D. K., Shi, Y. & Cardwell, D. A. The processing and properties of bulk (RE)BCO high temperature superconductors: current status and future perspectives. Supercond. Sci. Technol. 34, 053002 (2021)."),[11](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR11 "Namburi, D. K., Shi, Y., Dennis, A. R., Durrell, J. H. & Cardwell, D. A. A robust seeding technique for the growth of single grain (RE)BCO and (RE)BCO–Ag bulk superconductors. Supercond. Sci. Technol. 31, 044003 (2018).") show that the (Ba,Cu)-rich liquid phase from a bottom liquid source can fully infiltrate upwards into an upper Y211 pellet, driven by capillary forces. Therefore, most of the wetting melt is expected to remain within the porous Y211 skeleton, enabling single-crystal growth without slumping or collapse of the 3D-printed objects. Reports of 3D printed samples with partial liquid melt are found for other materials, such as liquid sintering in 3D-printed micro-lattices[33](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR33 "Zhang, D., Kenel, C. & Dunand, D. C. Microstructure and mechanical properties of 3D ink-extruded CoCrCuFeNi microlattices. Acta Mater. 238, 118187 (2022)."),[34](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR34 "Kenel, C., Al Malki, M. M. F. & Dunand, D. C. Microstructure evolution during reduction and sintering of 3D-extrusion-printed Bi2O3+TeO2 inks to form Bi2Te3. Acta Mater. 221, 117422 (2021).") and liquid infiltration in 3D-printed porous preforms[35](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#ref-CR35 "Zhang, D., Kenel, C., Caccia, M., Sandhage, K. H. & Dunand, D. C. Complex-shaped, finely-featured ZrC/W composites via shape-preserving reactive melt infiltration of porous WC structures fabricated by 3D ink extrusion. Addit. Manuf. Lett. 1, 100018 (2021)."), suggesting that many other materials may be amenable to seeded single-crystal growth after 3D printing.

The XRD patterns shown in Fig. [2c](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#Fig2) are collected on the top and bottom faces of the micro-lattice. Only the {00 l} family of peaks of the Y123 phase is observed, confirming that the samples are monocrystalline, with c-axis orientation. Figure [2d](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#Fig2) displays the IPF map and phase map near the center of the side face of the micro-lattice, showing that small Y211 particles are embedded in the Y123 matrix. The stitched IPF maps on the side face of the micro-lattice are shown in Fig. [2e](/articles/s41467-025-56708-x#Fig2). As the c-axis is aligned in the vertical direction, the side face shows the \(\{100\}/\{010\}\) orientation (however, the a and b axes cannot be differentiated from IPF maps due to their very close lattice parameter). # Printing a Superior Superconductor

## Additive manufacturing technique leads to ceramic-based superconductor materials with greater flexibility

#### The Problem

The ceramic properties of superconductor material make them brittle.

#### Our Idea

A method to print superconductors that will have more power.

#### Why It Matters

The method will help superconductor-powered inventions cost less, allowing them to make more impacts in numerous fields.

#### Our Team

Professor David Dunand, Dingchang Zhang (PhD ’24)

Superconductor materials have made a great difference in lives, from powering medical imaging for MRIs to allowing trains to float above tracks as they move, saving energy.

![David Dunand](../../../../../images/news/2025/02/david-dunand.jpg)

![David Dunand](../../../../../images/news/2025/02/david-dunand.jpg)

Yet there also has been a frustrating downside to more advanced superconductors: their ceramic property makes them brittle.

“Ceramic-based cuprates are common high-temperature superconductors, and, because they operate with liquid nitrogen, are immensely cheaper and easier to work with than low temperature metal superconductors,” said [David Dunand](https://www.mccormick.northwestern.edu/research-faculty/directory/profiles/dunand-david.html "Learn about David Dunand"), professor of materials science and engineering at the McCormick School of Engineering. “The shapes these materials can make, however, have been limited because of their brittleness. You’d like to be able to make complex objects that are optimized for energy efficiency.”

Dunand and collaborators from Fermilab are out to change this with the help of 3D printing, Their printing method printed superconductors that will have more power, which will help superconductor-powered inventions cost less.

The team’s work is outlined in the paper “[Additively-manufactured Monocrystalline YBCO Superconductor](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-56708-x "Read xAdditively-manufactured Monocrystalline YBCO Superconductor"),” published Feb. 24 in *Nature Communications*. Dunand was a co-corresponding author of the paper along with Dingchang Zhang (PhD ’24), a postdoctoral researcher at the University California, Berkeley, and a former student in [Dunand’s lab](https://www.dunand.northwestern.edu/ "Visit Dunand’s lab"). Cristian Boffo, PIP-II project manager at Fermilab, also co-authored the study.

![This is an image of the 3D printing of superconducting material.](../../../../../images/news/2025/02/printing-a-superior-superconductor2-body.jpg)

![This is an image of the 3D printing of superconducting material.](../../../../../images/news/2025/02/printing-a-superior-superconductor2-body.jpg)

### A breakthrough for a breakthrough

To not be limited by the brittleness of cuprates, the research team developed a method to successfully produce single-crystal YBCO — a common polycrystal superconductor —using additive manufacturing, commonly known as 3D printing.

Breaking down the process, the first step uses commercially available precursor powder to prepare ink. Ink is then put in a syringe that is extruded to create YBCO micro-lattices or other complex geometries that are polycrystals. The 3D-printed material later becomes a single crystal on 3D-printed parts by a melt growth method.

Normally, bulk superconductors are created in simple form by mold pressing. They are then sintered, or heated, and the pressed powders merge together. The researchers here used an ink where the powder is suspended and applied 3D printing to make it into a complex object for sintering.

The researchers were also able to remove the material’s grain boundaries — small defects in crystal structures that can lessen a material’s electrical and thermal conductivity. This presented a more effective superconducting current.

![Levitation](../../../../../images/news/2025/02/printing-a-superior-superconductor2-levitation.jpg)
![Micrographh](../../../../../images/news/2025/02/printing-a-superior-superconductor2-micrograph.jpg)

“People have made single crystals in a block of material, and we’ve shown we can use this same technique with 3D printing,” Zhang said. “During our process, we can fabricate complex shapes, such as toroidal coils, with a single crystal seed placed on top. Through a controlled processing window, these 3D-printed parts partially melt and transform into single crystals, retaining their original 3D-printed shape.”

“At Fermilab, we are developing the next-generation superconducting magnets that will drive scientific experiments for decades to come,” Boffo said. “The technology created through this collaboration will enable designs that were previously unimaginable, thereby enhancing our potential for advancement.”

"The single-crystal object can carry a greater amount of electrical current, making it able to provide more power, thus making magnets immensely stronger,” Dunand said. “This provides more energy for particle accelerators, such as FermiLab. The faster particles may in fact unlock new discoveries for physicists.”

### Super possibilities

According to Dunand, this work has the potential to only be the beginning of more powerful and efficient superconductors. The group plans to apply their method to other ceramic superconductor materials.  
  
“We did this research with YBCO, the most common superconductor, but there are many other compounds with even higher performance temperatures than can be processed by our method,” Dunand said. “The possible applications for this are very exciting.”

#### **Goal**: Develop an industry-wide consortium roadmapping effort to overcome the technological obstacles and accelerate growth of commercial manufacturing of high-temperature superconductors.

**Background**:  High Temperature Superconductors (HTS) have the potential to provide multiple commercial solutions to a variety of sectors in the U.S. economy such as energy, defense, industrial applications, communications, and medicine.

For example, in the energy sector HTS devices have the potential to accelerate the introduction of smart grid hardware applications and improve sustainability through enhanced energy efficiency, less emissions, better power quality, and improved resiliency and security of the power grid.  Superconducting devices do not simply provide improvements over conventional technologies; they provide unique solutions to challenges that cannot be achieved otherwise.  Meeting the challenges to growth of commercial HTS can yield significant economic and environmental advantages.

**Approach**:  The [Advanced Superconductor Manufacturing Institute](https://uh.edu/uh-research/asmi/) (ASMI) at the University of Houston held several workshops with over 50 organizations representing the industry, including manufacturers, end users, academics, and national laboratories.

The workshops assessed the challenges, barriers, aspirations, and risks of this technology in its many applications, which include power cables, rotating machinery, power grids, and magnets.  Goals of the project were to: 1. enable an industry-driven consortium to assess technological challenges; 2. address the "missing middle" in advanced HTS innovation to bridge the gap between manufacturing of prototypes and full-scale commercialization; 3. produce a roadmap for the industry and a business model for ASMI; and 4. expedite the transition of superconductor manufacturing to commercialization through cost reduction, high-volume production, reliability assurance, and effective integration into the existing infrastructure.

**Outcome:**  **The consortium developed an industry-wide roadmap to address the technological barriers to growth of high-temperature semiconductor commercial manufacturing**, while addressing the entire value chain and broad range of superconductor applications.  The roadmap identified the following targeted industry goals to mitigate the primary barriers to widespread commercial applications of HTS technology, including wire availability, wire cost, wire performance, and wire reliability:

# Fermilab-funded research demonstrates new method for 3D printing high-temperature superconductors

April 28, 2025
| By: [Madeleine O’Keefe](https://news.fnal.gov/authors/madeleine-okeefe/)

Tags:

This technique, published recently in Nature Communications, is the first to fabricate 3D-printed ceramic superconductors with a monocrystalline microstructure, advancing beyond the traditional polycrystalline microstructures.

Funding from the U.S. Department of Energy’s Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory has enabled researchers at [Northwestern University](https://www.mccormick.northwestern.edu/news/articles/2025/02/printing-a-superior-superconductor/) and Fermilab to invent a new process for 3D printing high-temperature superconductors. This method is the first to fabricate 3D-printed ceramic superconductors with a monocrystalline microstructure and is currently awaiting U.S. patent approval.

Their [paper](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-56708-x) was published Feb. 24 in *Nature Communications*.

![](https://news.fnal.gov/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/3d-print-sc-cristian-1-220x300.jpg)

Cristian Boffo holds a folded superconductor foil 3D printed from a single crystal of yttrium barium copper oxide.  
Credit: Melanie Turenne

[Superconductors](https://www.energy.gov/science/doe-explainssuperconductivity) are a special type of material that can conduct electricity with zero resistance — but only at extremely cold temperatures. High-temperature superconductors are unique because they work at temperatures greater than traditional superconductors, making them more practical for applications.

Cristian Boffo, a co-author on the paper, secured funding for the project through Fermilab’s Laboratory Directed Research and Development program. Boffo is project director for the Proton Improvement Plan-II, a project building a state-of-the-art [superconducting linear accelerator](https://pip2.fnal.gov/how-it-works/superconducting-radio-frequency-technology/) at Fermilab.

Boffo received LDRD funding to develop advanced magnets called superconducting undulators, aligning with activities planned in Fermilab’s Applied Physics and Superconducting Technology Directorate. One of the project’s aims is to explore new technologies that can revolutionize the design and manufacturing of superconducting undulators through the introduction of high-temperature superconductors and 3D printing. That’s where the Northwestern researchers came in.

“Fermilab wants to make better and better magnet systems that are more efficient and reach higher performance,” said Boffo. “Northwestern provides the experience in 3D printing, and we provide experience in superconductors.”

### The quest for high-temperature superconductors

When electricity flows through a material at normal temperatures, there will always be some energy loss due to resistance. Over 100 years ago, scientists discovered the phenomenon of superconductivity: when brought down to temperatures close to absolute zero — minus 460 degrees Fahrenheit (minus 273 degrees Celsius) — materials lose electrical resistance, enabling electricity to move through them extremely efficiently. This requires expensive and hard-to-handle coolants like liquid helium.

In the 1980s, scientists discovered high-temperature superconductors that will operate at a higher — albeit still frigid — critical temperature of minus 321 degrees Fahrenheit (minus 196 degrees Celsius). These are mostly ceramic materials, often copper oxides combined with other metallic oxides. These superconductors can be cooled with liquid nitrogen instead of liquid helium.

“This new technology will enable new magnet designs, leading to higher performances and potentially even allow the production of a new generation of superconducting radio-frequency cavities.”

Cristian Boffo

Fermilab

“Using liquid nitrogen, it’s much less expensive to cool down the structure to where it becomes superconducting,” said David Dunand, a professor of materials science and engineering at Northwestern University who performed the research.

Today, technological uses for superconductivity range from low-loss power generators to electric motors, from medical imaging technology to quick, quiet magnetically levitated trains. But superconductors’ need for extreme cold limits their usefulness, so developing high-temperature superconductors is vital for advancing everyday applications.

And in physics, high-temperature superconductors are especially advantageous because they retain their superconductivity in higher magnetic fields than traditional superconducting materials do.

### From polycrystals to monocrystal

To 3D print superconducting ceramics, scientists start with a “precursor powder”: a finely milled mix of chemical compounds. They combine the powder with a binder to create a printable paste, which can then be extruded by a 3D printer to build a structure, layer by layer — like the coil technique of making pottery. The structure is then heated and baked in a furnace, a high-temperature process known as sintering.

The resulting piece has a polycrystalline microstructure, but this is not ideal for creating or trapping a strong magnetic field. A monocrystalline superconductor would have much better properties for potential uses in accelerator physics, but it cannot be made via powder 3D printing.

So Dunand and his graduate student Dingchang Zhang sought to demonstrate, for the first time, a way to combine the superior physics properties of a monocrystalline superconductor with the complex architecture of a 3D-printed polycrystalline structure.

![A sample of a superconductor 3D printed from a single seed — this one shaped like a paper airplane — is shown levitating over a magnet. Credit: Dingchang Zhang](https://news.fnal.gov/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/3d-print-sc-levitation-1-897x505.jpg "3d print sc levitation")

A sample of a superconductor 3D printed from a single seed — this one shaped like a paper airplane — is shown levitating over a magnet. Credit: Dingchang Zhang

![This folded superconducting material was 3D printed from a single crystal of YBCO. Credit: Melanie Turenne](https://news.fnal.gov/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/3d-print-sc-ruler-1-1920x1080.jpg "3d print sc ruler")
![A superconducting material is 3D printed. Credit: Dingchang Zhang](https://news.fnal.gov/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/3d-print-sc-printing-1-1314x739.jpg "Screenshot")
![](https://news.fnal.gov/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/3D-print-sc-product-1-808x454.jpg "3D print sc product")

The new paper outlines their successful method. They 3D print a ceramic polycrystalline superconductor using common precursor powder mixture called yttrium barium copper oxide, or YBCO. Once it is sintered, researchers place a single-crystal seed made of a different superconducting material, neodymium barium copper oxide, or NdBCO, on top. They then begin a process called top seeded melt growth: heating the printed structure so it partially melts, filling holes or pores in the 3D-printed microstructure, making it more robust. Researchers then cool the structure very slowly so it re-solidifies with the same crystallographic orientation as the seed. The final piece retains its original 3D-printed shape, now with a stronger monocrystalline structure.

Dunand and Zhang used their method first with a 3D-printed micro-lattice shape, then graduated to more complex shapes. They found they could use a single seed to fabricate superconducting pieces up to 10 centimeters in diameter. They even used their method to 3D print a foil of superconducting material that Zhang folded into the shape of a tiny paper airplane, demonstrating that 3D-printed superconductors can be formed into complex shapes with sharp corners.

In the future, they hope to investigate multi-seed methods — different from the polycrystal method — in which they hope to produce larger pieces by using many separate single-crystal seeds.

“If we want to use it for accelerators, we need to print larger parts,” said Zhang, who completed his PhD at Northwestern in August 2024. “If we want to get bigger parts, how will we place the seeds? Whether that will have other problems, we don’t know.”

Dunand said their method “made a huge step forward” by showing it is possible to create monocrystal superconductors with complex shapes. They hope it also inspires further research into 3D printing monocrystal ceramics.

 “I think it’s highly, highly scalable,” said Dunand. If they could do it in their lab at Northwestern, Dunand said he thinks it can be replicated in many other settings.

“This new technology will enable new magnet designs, leading to higher performances and potentially even allow the production of a new generation of superconducting radio-frequency cavities,” said Boffo. “I think that this was a very successful collaboration.”

enriched_points.expanded_content: Additive manufacturing enables flexible and innovative designs for superconductors, overcoming traditional limitations by allowing intricate geometries and tailored microstructures for enhanced performance.

Nanostructuring techniques improve superconducting properties by enhancing critical current density and reducing grain boundary effects, thereby increasing the efficiency and functionality of superconducting materials.
Recent advancements in material synthesis, such as using precursor powders and controlled atmospheres, have led to the production of more efficient superconductors with higher critical temperatures.

Innovative manufacturing techniques significantly enhance superconducting properties, including critical current density and magnetic field tolerance, enabling broader applications in technology and energy sectors.

The integration of advanced manufacturing techniques holds promise for developing next-generation superconductors, with potential applications in energy storage, transportation, and quantum computing technologies.
